text: Anytime someone gives me the opportunity to be Guy Voller, I'm going to take that opportunity. It's just you can't pass that up.
I'm going to talk about peristomal hernia repair. In the middle of this, I actually have Guy's slides, and so we'll go into his topic kind of in the middle of my talk here as well.

These are my disclosures, which don't have any relevance to what we're going to talk about. I don't think I have to tell this audience that peristomal hernias are common. They affect up to fifty percent of stomas.

I think if you get CAT scans or look for people, you will find people with non-symptomatic peristomal hernias at a much higher percentage than that. We know that these are technically difficult to repair, that they do have a higher surgical site occurrence rate, a higher recurrence rate as well.

And you can add into this the problems of stoma-related complications such as obstruction at the stoma site, injury to the stoma site, necrosis, and kinking from stoma repairs. We also know that midline hernias are very, very commonly found with these as well. So when you put this constellation together, this is a problem that we do not have yet a very good solution to.

There's lots of options for repairing these with mesh. We do know that mesh-based repairs are the preferred way to fix these. Keyhole repair can be done as an intra-abdominal, an onlay, or as a retromuscular repair. You can sugar baker the mesh, and for a while I think sugar baker was confined to the intraperitoneal space. We're going to show you a little bit at the end how we've moved that into the retromuscular plane.
You can also cruciate the mesh in any number of locations, and I'll touch on that a little bit as part of Guy's talk. My repair algorithm for peristomals is basically the following.

I reserve laparoscopic sugar baker repairs for those that are relatively small peristomal hernias or that have a smaller or minimal midline component. Once somebody has a large peristomal hernia or has a moderate or large midline hernia in combination with it, I prefer to do a posterior component separation, typically using a transversus abdominis release.

Your options for doing that include moving the ostomy to a new location or cruciating the mesh or utilizing a stapled ostomy reinforcement procedure. If the ostomy can't be relocated, you can keyhole the mesh around it, but literature supports not keyholing meshes as often as possible.

And what I'm going to talk about today is sort of our newest modification of this algorithm, which is to intentionally leave the ostomy where it is presently located and retromuscularize it utilizing a PPHR technique.

So how do we do a TAR with an ostomy in sight? Well, the first thing you have to realize is you've seen several videos of TARs here. When you do a TAR and there's an ostomy that you're taking down, there will be a defect in the posterior layer that's just part and parcel of the operation, and you simply sew that up.
The surgeon begins in the upper abdomen to release the transversus abdominis muscle, a step that is visually clearer in robotic procedures due to enhanced visibility compared to open techniques.

We typically, in the older version of this operation, would move the ostomy to a new location. So we close the old stoma site in the posterior sheath, we move the ostomy to a new site, and you create a small cruciate in the posterior layer of the reconstruction. to deliver the stoma through. You then put mesh in place and you created a cruciate in the mesh to deliver the stoma through that site. And this is a on-floss view of what that repair looks like.

The video shows the old ostomy site on the left-hand side, which is reinforced with mesh. The midline is also reinforced with mesh, but the mesh has been cut to allow the new ostomy to pass through. A cross-sectional view illustrates the mesh cut near the midline for the new ostomy, highlighting potential risks of compromising the midline repair and hernia formation at the new ostomy site.

Mike Rosen and Yuri Novitskiy published their initial experience with this technique. Seventy percent of their patients in this series had a midline incisional hernia as well, which gave them a very large hernia area of almost two hundred and thirty centimeters in these patients. At thirteen months, they had eleven percent recurrence rate, and most of those recurrences were located at the new ostomy site. Whether you want to call that a new hernia or a recurrent hernia, it's neither here nor there. The patient had a peristomal hernia, and they have a new peristomal hernia that requires a repair.

They had the anticipated amount of postoperative problems, including deep and superficial surgical site infections. They had one mucocutaneous separation. Again, these are problems inherent to managing people who have an ostomy. And I will point out that they had one patient with an ischemic stoma in that series. But I think ultimately that repair gives you a very acceptable risk of recurrence. It does relocate the ostomy. The new site is reinforced. You get a Y sublay overlap. You can repair midline defects, and I think there's pretty good support that it's safe to use polypropylene mesh in those circumstances.

The surgeon moves the ostomy to a new location, creating both an old and new wound site. The bowel is transected and mobilized to the new site, with visible contamination likely due to the relocation. The mesh is cut to accommodate the new ostomy site, which is a common location for hernia formation.

The surgeon attempts to align the mesh configuration by creating a hole in the posterior sheath, mesh, and new ostomy site in the native abdominal wall. This alignment is challenging, and techniques such as stapled reinforcement or smart techniques are considered to facilitate mesh placement and prevent hernia recurrence.

The video shows the use of an EEA stapler to prevent mesh migration at the new ostomy site. A small hole is made in the subcutaneous layer to facilitate the technique, ensuring the mesh and abdominal wall remain aligned without kinking or sliding during the healing process.

They use the anvil of what essentially is an EEA stapler. The meshes fired into that space in the bowel delivered through the middle of the mesh in the trophene made in the abdominal wall. There have been subsequent reports on this. In hernia in two thousand fifteen, Williams reported twenty-two patients. There were a combination of old and new ostomy sites. With a median follow-up of twenty-one months, they reported nineteen percent paralysomal hernia recurrence with the SMART technique and seventy-three percent if you just relocated.

So good evidence for using this to prophylax. In a more recent study in two thousand seventeen, fourteen patients had the same technique prophylaxis for colon sites that were created. They had two recurrences with a median follow up of two years.

I personally wrote a little rebuttal to this article just sort of criticizing the technique a little bit. I kind of disagree with using a stapler to resect portions of the native abdominal wall. And while the idea that stapling the mesh to the abdominal wall helps it and theoretically prevents that size from being open, I can assure you that that is not the case. This is a patient from my institution. I have a surgeon who'd been doing this technique long before the original descriptions of it. He simply never published it. And so this is onlay polypropylene mesh, and this was a twenty-one millimeter EEA stapler site, which now measures almost four millimeters in size, four centimeters in size. and now has a peristomal hernia associated with it. So the staples, they're not interconnected.

They don't prevent that ostomy site from expanding over the course of time. And so I don't know in the long term whether this here is not a good idea. Yuri Novitskiy has published utilizing a TAR technique with what he described as a STORM or a stapled transabdominal ostomy reinforcement. This is essentially the way, instead of cruciating the mesh where the new ostomy is going to be located with that TAR procedure that I showed you earlier, of rather utilizing the stapler to create that trophene.

So these are Yuri's slides. I'm giving guys talk with Yuri's slides. I kind of like this. The tar is performed in the upper abdomen, similar to the technique you've seen earlier. The bowel is brought through the posterior sheath and delivered at the site that will be planned for the new ostomy location. You then align all of the layers, and again, this is really where the sort of art of this repair comes in place.

The surgeon aligns the three layers of the abdominal wall, ensuring they are properly positioned before creating openings for bowel delivery. An anvil is placed through the posterior sheath and mesh, connecting through the abdominal wall. The stapler is fired, allowing the bowel to be delivered through the aligned layers at the new ostomy location.
The video shows synthetic mesh stapled to the abdominal wall in an onlay position, with fibrin sealant applied. The bowel is delivered through the stapled reinforcement of the onlay trophene.

Whether or not this prevents recurrences, I don't know. But if you're doing a TAR and are moving the ostomy to a new location, I think it is a very nice way to make sure that those three layers are lined up, you know, in a very precise fashion, that they don't have the opportunity to migrate or shift in the very early postoperative period. So I think for that purpose, it clearly makes sense.

What we have described, and I think what other people are doing now, is essentially a tar, like I showed you, but rather than moving the stoma, the stoma is retromuscularized, utilizing what is essentially a sugar baker configuration. So this is a tar and a sugar baker put together into one operation. The mesh does not need to be cut. It's a sugar baker configuration mesh. The stoma does not need to be relocated. So it addresses some of the problems that we described earlier with moving the ostomy and doing a tar. I will say that having done a fair number of these now, it's very difficult to configure the mesh around the bowel. and there are some pretty severe consequences to doing that poorly, as I'll show you at the end here.

The video shows a cadaver model with a retromuscular dissection performed. A retrorectus release is completed while leaving the ostomy in place, followed by a transversus abdominis release around the stoma. The procedure is demonstrated in an open approach for clarity.

The surgeon carefully avoids injuring the linea semilunaris and the ostomy while intentionally creating a defect in the posterior sheath. This allows the bowel to be moved from a medial location in the posterior sheath to a lateral position in the preperitoneal or pre-transversalis space. The defect is then sutured from lateral to medial.

The surgeon places the bowel in a Sugarbaker configuration, with the peritoneum visible below it. The posterior rectus sheath is closed in the midline, creating a large space for mesh placement in the sublay space. Various mesh options are displayed.
The surgeon places the mesh far lateral without trans-fascial fixation, using either fibrin sealant or no fixation to keep it in situ, minimizing the risk of bowel creeping around the sides.

The surgeon demonstrates an operative case using intermediate-weight polypropylene mesh in a sugar baker configuration. The mesh provides a wide overlap at the midline defect without being cut near the midline. A cross-sectional view shows the bowel entering the retromuscular plane on the patient's right side, visible on the screen's left, and ascending in a sugar baker configuration towards the midline. An on FOSS view illustrates the overall configuration.

The surgeon performs a robotic transversus abdominis release (TAR) around the ostomy, having already taken down the posterior sheath. The posterior layer is closed after lateralizing the ostomy. The surgeon measures the mesh size, ensuring adequate overlap with the peristomal defect, and places lateralizing trans-fascial fixation sutures on both the right and left sides.

The mesh is positioned in situ in a Sugarbaker configuration. The robotic system is placed on the ipsilateral side to the ostomy. The surgeon performs a TAR on the opposite side and unrolls the mesh to cover the peristomal and midline hernias.

The video shows a comparison of pre-procedure and post-procedure images. In the post-procedure image at thirty days, the bowel is seen entering the retromuscular plane at a shallow angle. At one year follow-up, the colon is observed entering the retromuscular plane at a similar shallow angle with no visible hernia recurrence.

The video shows a patient with a large midline defect and a small peristomal defect. The midline defect is visible before and after closure, with the stoma site lateralized and reinforced. The post-operative images show the patient a year after the repair.

You know, there's a lot of things about this patient that make it a challenging patient. It's a Crohn's patient with a multiply recurrent hernia. You know, ultimately, mesh configuration around any ostomy can result in problems, and this operation is ultimately no different than that. So I think before everybody goes home and starts doing this, you know, the jury is still out on this as well, just like a guy concluded I'm going to conclude the same thing. This may be a good idea, this may be a really terrible idea, but I'm going to leave this slide up so that we all at least recognize that it may be a problem.
Thank you very much.
